scenario: Team wonders difference between DMN element description (attribute on most elements) and annotation column (in decision tables).
question: DMN annotation vs description?

options.text: The description attribute and the annotation column serve different documentation purposes in DMN at different scopes. The description is a metadata attribute on DMN model elements (Decision, Business Knowledge Model, Input Data, Knowledge Source) that explains the element's overall purpose in the decision requirements diagram. The annotation column (annotations) in a decision table is a per-rule text field that explains the business rationale for that specific rule row. Both are purely documentary and ignored by the evaluation engine, but they operate at element level vs rule level respectively, and are distinct fields in the DMN XML schema.

The annotation column in DMN decision tables is a standard feature of the DMN specification and remains fully supported in Camunda 8. It provides per-rule documentation within a decision table, explaining the business rationale or regulatory reference for each rule. Far from being deprecated, annotation columns are encouraged as a best practice for maintaining readable, auditable decision models. Removing annotation support would eliminate valuable inline documentation for complex decision tables with many rules and business context.

The description attribute in DMN is a documentation element with no runtime effect. The DMN engine ignores descriptions entirely during decision evaluation; they do not influence input matching, rule selection, or output computation. Description is purely metadata for human readers, displayed in modeling tools and documentation generators but never interpreted as logic. Conflating documentation with executable logic would suggest descriptions could alter decision results, which would be incorrect and potentially dangerous.

Different documentation concepts: the description attribute is element-level metadata applied to Decision, BKM, Input Data, and Knowledge Source nodes in a DRD, explaining the overall purpose of each model element. The annotation column appears within a decision table and provides per-rule free-text commentary explaining why a particular rule exists -- for example, citing a regulatory reference or business policy behind that specific condition-outcome combination. Both are ignored by the DMN evaluation engine and carry no computational meaning. Their scope differs: description documents the model element; annotation documents an individual rule. Using them consistently improves auditability and maintainability of complex decision models.

a.text: Incorrect. DMN documentation features: Description attribute: Scope: element-level. Applicable elements: Decision. BKM. Decision Table. Input Data. Knowledge Source. Most DMN model elements. In XML: `xml Decides whether to approve a loan application based on credit score, income, and existing debt. Owner: lending-team@example.com ... Visible: Property panel in modelers. Hover tooltips (in some tools). Generated docs. Annotation column (per Set 13 Q25): Scope: per-rule in Decision Tables. Purpose: Explains why a specific rule exists. Business / regulatory context. In XML: xml ... ... Per regulation X requiring this case Verify exact element structure per DMN version. Visible: Inline column in decision table view. Per-rule. Differences: | Aspect | Description | Annotation | |--------|-------------|------------| | Scope | Element-level | Rule-level (in table) | | Where | XML attribute / child | Table column | | What | Element purpose | Rule rationale | | Engine impact | None | None | Both documentary: Engine ignores. For human readers. Improve maintainability. Best practices: Both populated for production DMN. Description: element purpose. Annotation: per-rule rationale. For regulatory compliance: Description: cite regulations affecting the decision. Annotation: link specific rules to regulation clauses. For team knowledge: Description: owner, contact, history. Annotation: tribal knowledge per rule. For audit: Comprehensive documentation aids audit. Clear rationale per rule. For evolution: When rules change: update annotation. When element changes: update description. Other documentation features: label attribute: Display label (vs name and id). For visualisation. External docs links: Knowledge Source in DRD: links to external docs. href` to URL: documentation references. Anti-patterns: Empty descriptions: missed opportunity. Outdated descriptions: misleading. No annotation on complex rules: cryptic. For tooling: Web Modeler: shows both prominently. DMN linter (custom): can enforce non-empty descriptions. For documentation generation: Extract descriptions + annotations → Confluence / Markdown. Per element / per rule. For the scenario (description vs annotation): Description: about the element overall. Annotation: about specific rule. Use both for comprehensive docs.
